ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for milkshake; A0FKP (Milk-based drinks) is the closest defensible broader category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for milkshake as a food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium cup of milkshake (approximately 300 g) is a typical single-serve portion as commonly consumed.

nutrient_source.title: Milkshake — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for vanilla milkshake (NDB 01110) as a secondary cross-reference and documented below.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 31 for milkshake is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database entries for dairy-based blended beverages containing ice cream and milk. The input record cited GI 31 (low) with GL 2.1 per 100g. GL per serving recalculated: carbohydrate_g per 300g serving = 20.58g; GL = (31 × 20.58) / 100 = 6.38, rounded to 6.3. GI value accepted from input with University of Sydney GI Database as supporting authority for dairy-based milkshake category.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (6.1 g/100g) from ice cream and flavoured syrup independently elevates blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 23 mg/100g but does not offset the SSB sugar burden.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid milkshakes due to the added sugar load from ice cream and syrup, which independently raises cardiovascular and blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; substitute with plain low-fat milk or an unsweetened fruit-and-milk smoothie to retain calcium and potassium without added sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Milkshake is a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) with 6.1 g sugar/100g from added ice cream and flavoured syrup; liquid sugar form accelerates glycaemic response and triggers the SSB hard gate for diabetes management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid milkshakes as the liquid added-sugar matrix from ice cream and syrup causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation; replace with plain milk or a smoothie made with unsweetened yogurt and whole fruit to provide calcium without the SSB sugar burden.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~1.6 g/100g) from ice cream and whole milk combined with added sugar (6.1 g/100g) contributes to atherogenic lipid profile and endothelial dysfunction risk with regular consumption.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit milkshake consumption due to the combined saturated fat and added sugar content; if dairy is desired, choose plain low-fat milk or a smoothie with no added sugar to minimise LDL-raising saturated fat and sugar-driven triglyceride elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar from ice cream and flavoured syrup promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; minimal dietary fibre (0.48 g/100g) provides no anti-inflammatory buffering.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid milkshakes as the added sugar content promotes systemic inflammation; replace with plain milk or a smoothie enriched with anti-inflammatory ingredients such as ginger, turmeric, or berries.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A milkshake is a thick, cold blended beverage made by combining whole milk and ice cream, typically with a flavoured syrup such as chocolate, vanilla, or strawberry. The result is a smooth, creamy, sweet drink with a rich dairy flavour and a dense, pourable consistency. Milkshakes are widely consumed as a dessert beverage or snack across Western and international food cultures, and are commonly served in fast-food restaurants, diners, and cafes. Per 100g, a milkshake provides approximately 56 kcal, 1.25g protein, 2.64g fat (of which approximately 1.6g is saturated), and 6.86g carbohydrate including 6.08g sugar. Sodium is low at 23mg/100g, and calcium is present at 38mg/100g from the dairy base. Dietary fibre is minimal at 0.48g/100g. The glycaemic index is low at 31, but the liquid sugar matrix from added ice cream and syrup means postprandial glucose response is faster than solid foods with equivalent GI. Clinically, milkshakes are classified as sugar-sweetened beverages (SSBs) and are unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to the combined added sugar and saturated fat burden. They are vegetarian and gluten-free but contain dairy allergens and are not suitable for vegans or those who are lactose intolerant.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a milkshake?
What is the sugar content of milkshake?
Can I drink milkshake if I have heart disease?
What is the GI of milkshake?
Is milkshake high in saturated fat?
What is a healthier alternative to milkshake?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and minor fatty acid fractions cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy vanilla milkshake entry. GI value of 31 consistent with University of Sydney GI Database dairy-beverage category. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible.
notes: Saturated fat (1.60g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.76g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.10g/100g), potassium_mg (80.0mg/100g), and cholesterol_mg (10.0mg/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy vanilla milkshake (NDB 01110) as HPB did not report these fields for this entry; values are nutritionally plausible and consistent with the dairy-ice cream composition. trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data not reported by HPB for this milkshake entry and not cross-referenced from USDA SR Legacy at this time; these fields require a dedicated micronutrient lookup for a future enrichment pass. Milkshake is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to the presence of added sugar from ice cream and flavoured syrup. The dietary_tags 'low_calorie' from the input record are retained as contextual tags only; clinically this food is not recommended for SSB-sensitive conditions. GL per serving recalculated at 300g serving size: (31 × 20.58g carbohydrate) / 100 = 6.38, rounded to 6.3.
